mcnemar.reconstruction_note: Per-instance BON outcomes reconstructed from predict_p_correct_by_T using BON threshold p>0.0424 (P_BON>0.5 with N=16). Reconstructed k: T_op_0.3=26, fixed_T07=26, fixed_T10=28 (reported: 27, 25, 28 respectively).
mcnemar.mandatory_caveat: The 6.7 pp difference (0 instance(s) net advantage out of 30) is directionally consistent with the Arrhenius prediction but does not reach statistical significance (McNemar p=1.000, n=30); confirming an effect of this size at 80% power requires approximately n≈200 valid instances.

bootstrap_R2.interpretation: Median R²=0.848 (95% bootstrap CI: [0.754, 0.914]); the C1 threshold of 0.85 is 0.002 above the observed median, which falls within bootstrap estimation error — the binary met/not-met judgment is statistically indeterminate.

power_analysis.interpretation: At n=30, the minimum detectable ρ with 80% power (two-sided α=0.05) is approximately ρ_min≈0.49; the observed ρ=0.674 substantially exceeds this, confirming adequate power. The C6 finding is not underpowered.

t_turn_reconciliation.clarification: T_TURN^theory = Ea/ln(K) (mean≈0.24, median≈0.15, IQR≈[0.07,0.30]) is the theoretical upper bound quantity in Theorem 6, proving window [T_thresh, T_TURN^theory] is non-empty when N>K; it is distinct from T_TURN^emp = Du et al. entropy inflection point (mean≈1.22, median≈1.40, IQR≈[1.32,1.40]). The 93.3% window fraction uses T_TURN^emp; Theorem 6 proves a complementary mathematical bound using T_TURN^theory. These quantities are not interchangeable and validate different aspects of the framework.

delta_approx_note.methodological_note: The delta-approx strategy (T_operating from single logprob call, accuracy=86.7%) achieves 3.3 pp less than regression-Ea T_operating (accuracy=90.0%). These are compared via a methodology asymmetry: regression Ea uses ~350 API calls per instance (7 temperatures × 50 samples) while delta-approx uses 1 logprob call. The accuracy difference is therefore confounded with information cost.

verdict_updates.C1_rationale: Observed median R²=0.848, 95% bootstrap CI [0.754, 0.914]. Threshold 0.85 falls within CI → verdict is INDETERMINATE.
verdict_updates.C7_rationale: McNemar p=1.000 (n=30); difference not statistically significant. Directionally consistent but confirmation requires n≈200.
verdict_updates.C6_rationale: At n=30, the minimum detectable ρ with 80% power (two-sided α=0.05) is approximately ρ_min≈0.49; the observed ρ=0.674 substantially exceeds this, confirming adequate power. The C6 finding is not underpowered.

examples.input:
What is the term for a sub-optimal but acceptable outcome of negotiations between parties?

A. Accepting
B. Settling
C. Negotiating
D. Mediating
E. Bargaining
F. Satisficing
G. Conceding
H. Collaborating
I. Arbitrating
J. Compromising

The primary objective of team-based selling is to

A. equitably manage the sales bonus pool
B. increase the competitiveness within the sales team
C. create multiple points of contact between the customer and the company
D. distribute the work involved in managing an account
E. reduce the workload of individual salespeople
F. ensure that every team member gets to interact with the customer
G. diversify the company's customer base
H. match various functional areas of the company with customer needs
I. simplify the process of sales negotiation
J. provide a single point of contact for the customer with the company

During a riot, Mr. Winter's car was overturned causing $346.50 in damage.Mr. Winter had $50-deductible col-lision insurance, but no comprehensive coverage. How much will theinsurance company pay Mr. Winter?

A. $296.50
B. $50
C. Two-thirds of the cost of the damages
D. $150
E. not pay him anything
F. $200
G. $346.50
H. $246.50
I. full coverage of damage
J. Half the cost of the damages

A state law required vacant public school buildings to be sold at auction to the highest bidder. A church in a city located in the state wanted to acquire larger facilities for its school. The city's school board proposed a new state law that would authorize it to sell a vacant public school building, at a price below its fair market value, to the church for use as its school. If enacted, would this law be constitutional?

A. No, because the law would be favoring one religious institution over others, thereby violating the First Amendment.
B. No, because the law would violate the separation of church and state as stipulated in the First Amendment.
C. No, because a statute specially authorizing the sale of the building without competitive bidding would violate the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
D. Yes, because the law would not primarily or substantially support the religious activities of the church.
E. No, because the law would infringe on the rights of other potential bidders who could offer a higher price for the building.
F. Yes, because the church's use of the building for educational purposes would not constitute an establishment of religion.
G. Yes, because the law would be a fair and equitable solution to a local problem.
H. No, because the transfer of the building to the church under these circumstances would constitute an establishment of religion prohibited by the First and Fourteenth Amendments.
I. Yes, because the law would be supporting education, not religion.
J. Yes, because surplus government property is not subject to the limitations imposed by the establishment clause as incorporated into the establishment clause as incorporated into the Fourteenth Amendment.

A debtor owed a creditor $12,000 under a promissory note. Under the applicable statute of limitations, a suit to collect on the promissory note had to have been filed by September 30 of last year. On June 1 of this year, the creditor received a letter from the debtor stating, "I shall pay you $5,000 on July 1 in full satisfaction of what I owe you. " However, the debtor failed to make the payment on July 1. If, on August 1, the creditor brings suit against the debtor and the debtor asserts the statute of limitations as an affirmative defense and refuses to pay the creditor anything, which of the following accurately states the creditor's legal rights against the debtor?

A. On July 1, not June 1, the creditor will be entitled to a judgment against the debtor for$12,000.
B. On June 1, the creditor became entitled to a judgment against the debtor for $12,000.
C. The creditor is entitled to a judgment against the debtor for $5,000, regardless of the date.
D. On August 1, the creditor became entitled to a judgment against the debtor for $12,000.
E. The creditor is entitled to a judgment against the debtor for $7,000, the remaining amount after the promised $5,000 payment.
F. On July 1, the creditor became entitled to a judgment against the debtor for the full $12,000, due to the debtor's failure to pay.
G. The creditor is not entitled to anything, due to the statute of limitations expiring.
H. On July 1, not June 1, the creditor became entitled to a judgment against the debtor for $5,000 only.
I. The creditor is not entitled to anything, on either June 1 or on July 1.

The owner of a parcel of land received the following letter from a buyer: "I will pay you $2,200 an acre for [theparcel]." The owner's letter of reply stated, "I accept your offer." Unknown to the owner, the buyer had intendedto offer only $2,000 per acre but had mistakenly typed "$2,200." As both parties knew, comparable land in thevicinity had been selling at prices between $2,000 and $2,400 per acre. Which of the following states the probable legal consequences of the correspondence between theparties?

A. There is a contract formed at a price of $2,400 per acre, as it is the highest price in the vicinity.
B. There is a contract, but the price per acre will be determined by a third-party appraiser.
C. There is no contract, because the parties attached materially different meanings to the price term.
D. There is no enforceable contract, because the buyer is entitled to rescission due to a mutual mistake asto a basic assumption.
E. There is no contract, because the buyer can argue that the price was a typo.
F. There is a contract formed at a price of $2,200 per acre, but the buyer can renegotiate the price.
G. There is no contract, because the parties have not met in person to discuss the deal.
H. There is a contract formed at a price of $2,000 per acre, as it is the lowest price in the vicinity.
I. There is a contract formed at a price of $2,000 per acre, as the buyer intended.
J. There is a contract formed at a price of $2,200 per acre, regardless of the buyer's true intention.

A man was at a bar drinking beer when he started conversing with a woman who was seated at the next barstool. During the course of their conversation, the woman told the man that she was just laid off her job and desperately needed money to pay her rent. The man, a practical joker, pointed to a nearby coat rack and said, "Hey, see that fur coat there. Why don't you take it?" The man then told the woman that he would cause a disturbance to distract attention while she ran out of the bar with the coat. Believing that it was a good idea, the woman agreed to take part in the scheme. Thereupon, the man lit a matchbook and threw it on top of the bar. He then yelled, "The bar's on fire, help!" When everyone turned to look at the man, the woman ran to the back of the bar, took the fur coat and scurried outside unnoticed. Just as the woman left the bar and was running down the sidewalk, she was apprehended and arrested by a police officer. Later, the man confessed that the fur coat that the woman took really belonged to him. With respect to the man's and the woman's criminal liability, which of the following is most correct?

A. The man and the woman are guilty of conspiracy.
B. The woman is guilty of conspiracy and larceny.
C. The man and the woman are guilty of larceny.
D. Neither the man nor the woman are guilty of larceny, but they are guilty of conspiracy.
E. The man is guilty of conspiracy and the woman is guilty of larceny.
F. The man is guilty of conspiracy.
G. The man and the woman are guilty of conspiracy and larceny.
H. The man and the woman are not guilty of either conspiracy or larceny.
I. The man is guilty of larceny.
J. The woman is guilty of larceny.

Vicarious liability is most likely to be an issue when a psychologist is acting in which of the following capacities?

A. Researcher
B. therapist
C. faculty member
D. mentor
E. Student
F. Consultant
G. Guest speaker
H. supervisor
I. Test administrator
J. Mediator

Which sentence most accurately describes how neurons communicate?

A. Messages are transmitted from neuron to neuron via sound waves.
B. Neurons send messages up the spinal cord to the cerebral cortex through neural reflexes.
C. Electricity passes between neurons, which carries messages between one neuron and another.
D. Neurons transmit information through the exchange of genetic material.
E. Neurons communicate through vibrations in the cellular membrane.
F. Neurons communicate through a combination of electrical and chemical signals, but without any physical contact.
G. Neurons communicate by emitting light signals that are received by neighboring neurons.
H. Neurons communicate through physical contact between dendrites of one cell and the next cell.
I. Neurons send messages to each other through the circulatory system.
J. Chemicals travel from one neuron to another, influencing whether a neuron will fire or not.

Describe the different techniques used in behavior therapy.

A. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are hypnotherapy, play therapy, interpersonal therapy, and motivational interviewing.
B. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are mindfulness, dialectical behavior therapy, psychodynamic therapy, and family therapy.
C. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are psychoanalysis, cognitive therapy, humanistic therapy, and group therapy.
D. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are bioenergetics, transactional analysis, reality therapy, and neuro-linguistic programming.
E. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are systematic desensitization, assertive training, aversive conditioning, and cognitive restructuring.
F. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are counterconditioning, operant conditioning, cognitive restructuring, and art therapy.
G. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are meditation, exposure therapy, dream analysis, and biofeedback.
H. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are counterconditioning, operant conditioning, modeling, and cognitive restructuring.
I. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are flooding, implosive therapy, token economies, and rational emotive behavior therapy.
J. The four different techniques of behavior therapy are gestalt therapy, narrative therapy, solution-focused brief therapy, and relaxation training.

Which of the following is a frameshift mutation?

A. CAT HAS HIS → CAT HIS HAT
B. CAT HAS HIS → CAT HATH IS
C. CAT HAS HIS → CAT WAS HIT
D. CAT HAS HIS → CAT HSH ISA
E. CAT HAS HIS → CAT SAW HIS
F. CAT HAS HIS → CAT HAS HIS CAT

Differentiate betweenisolecithal,telolecithal, and centrolecithal eggs. Which organisms are characteristicof each ?

A. Isolecithal eggs have a small, central yolk, found in some invertebrates and fish. Telolecithal eggs have yolk distributed in a gradient from one pole to the other, found in some amphibians. Centrolecithal eggs have multiple yolk pockets, found in certain crustaceans.
B. Isolecithal eggs are found in birds and reptiles, Telolecithal eggs are found in arthropods, Centrolecithal eggs are found in mollusks and echinoderms.
C. Isolecithal eggs are found in mammals, Telolecithal eggs are found in amphibians, Centrolecithal eggs are found in fish.
D. Isolecithal eggs are found in some species of fish, Telolecithal eggs are characteristic of most mammals, Centrolecithal eggs are typical of certain invertebrates like cephalopods.
E. Isolecithal eggs are large with yolk in layers, found in reptiles and birds. Telolecithal eggs are small with yolk in the center, found in arthropods. Centrolecithal eggs are moderate in size with yolk at one pole, found in mammals.
F. Isolecithal eggs are found in arthropods, Telolecithal eggs are found in annelids, mollusks, and echinoderms, Centrolecithal eggs are found in birds and reptiles.
G. Isolecithal eggs are evenly distributed with yolk, found in annelids, mollusks, and echinoderms. Telolecithal eggs have yolk concentrated toward one pole, found in reptiles and birds. Centrolecithal eggs have yolk massed toward the center, found in arthropods.
H. Isolecithal eggs have minimal yolk, found in echinoderms and some fish. Telolecithal eggs have a large amount of yolk throughout, found in most mammals. Centrolecithal eggs have yolk in a ring around the cell, found in amphibians.
I. Isolecithal eggs have yolk at one extreme end, found in some marine invertebrates. Telolecithal eggs have evenly dispersed small yolk granules, found in lower vertebrates. Centrolecithal eggs have a central yolk nucleus, found in certain worms.
J. Isolecithal eggs have yolk concentrated at the animal pole, found in amphibians and fish. Telolecithal eggs have a moderate amount of yolk evenly distributed, found in mammals. Centrolecithal eggs have yolk surrounding the cytoplasm, found in annelids and mollusks.

The van der Waal equation is a modification of the ideal gas equation. It reads [P + (an^2 / V^2)] (V - nb) = nRT where P = pressure, V = volume, n = number of moles, R = gas constant, T = absolute temperature, and (a) and (b) are constants for a particular gas. The term an^2 /V^2 corrects the pressure for intermolecular attraction and the term - nb corrects the volume for molecular volume. Using this equation, determine whether a gas becomes more or less ideal when: (a.) the gas is compressed at constant temperature; (b.) more gas is added at constant volume and temperature; and (c.) The temperature of the gas is raised at constant volume.

A. more ideal, less ideal, more ideal
B. less ideal, more ideal, less ideal
C. more ideal, less ideal, less ideal
D. closer to being ideal, more ideal, less ideal
E. less ideal, more ideal, more ideal
F. less ideal, less ideal, less ideal
G. more ideal, more ideal, less ideal
H. less ideal, less ideal, closer to being ideal
I. more ideal, less ideal, closer to being ideal
J. closer to being ideal, less ideal, more ideal

The solid-state structures of the principal allotropes of elemental boron are made up of which of the following structural units?

A. B5 pentagons
B. B10 decagons
C. B4 tetrahedra
D. B3 triangles
E. B11 hendecahedra
F. B9 nonagons
G. B8 cubes
H. B7 heptagons
I. B12 icosahedra
J. B6 octahedra

The 1H spectrum of a mixture of dimethylsulphoxide (DMSO) and acetonitrile (AN) contains lines with relative intensities α and 3α, respectively. What is the ratio of the two concentrations, [DMSO]:[AN]?

A. 3:2
B. 1:6
C. 1:9
D. 1:1
E. 2:3
F. 1:3
G. 3:1
H. 2:1
I. 6:1
J. 1:2

Most transition metals share a common oxidation state of +2. Which of the following best explains why?

A. Transition metals will gain their outermost s-block electrons when forming bonds.
B. Transition metals tend to gain electrons when reacting with other elements.
C. Transition metals tend to lose electrons when reacting with other elements.
D. Transition metals all have a minimum of two unpaired electrons.
E. Transition metals have a maximum of two unpaired electrons.
F. Transition metals will lose their outermost s-block electrons when forming bonds.
G. Transition metals will share their outermost s-block electrons when forming bonds.
H. Transition metals have a +2 charge in their ground state.
I. Transition metals have a stable configuration and are not reactive.
J. Transition metals have unstable configurations and are very reactive.

This question refers to the following information.
"The Chinese are all infidels: they worship images, and burn their dead just like the [Hindus]. The King of China is a Tartar, and one of the descendants of [Ghenghis] Khan…In all the Chinese provinces, there is a town for the [Muslims], and in this they reside. They also have cells, colleges, and mosques, and are made much of by the Kings of China…
When we approached this city [of Hangzhou] we were met by its judge, the [elders] of Islamism, and the great merchants. The [Muslims] are exceedingly numerous here. This whole city is surrounded by a wall: each of the six [districts of Hangzhou] is also surrounded by a wall. In the first reside the guards, with their commander. I was told that, in the muster-rolls, these amount to twelve thousand…In the second division are the Jews, Christians, and the Turks who worship the sun: these are numerous, their number is not known: and theirs is the most beautiful city. Their streets are well disposed, and their great men are exceedingly wealthy. There are in the city a great number of [Muslims], with some of whom I resided for fifteen days; and was treated most [honorably]…"
Ibn Battuta, Voyages, 1332–1346 C.E.
Compared to the observations expressed in the excerpt, Mongol administration of its Russian domains in the period 600 C.E. to 1450 C.E. differed in that it

A. resulted in the isolation of Russian territories, while the Mongol administration in China facilitated international trade and communication
B. was only tolerant of Orthodox Christianity, while the Mongol administration in China favored many religious traditions
C. relied heavily on tribute from the Russian principalities, while the Mongol administration in China emulated a centralized Chinese bureaucratic state
D. granted autonomy to local rulers, while the Mongol administration in China imposed direct control
E. imposed Islamic law in its domains, while the Mongol administration in China allowed for diverse religious practices
F. was viewed favorably by its subjects, while the Mongol administration in China encountered immense domestic unrest
G. led to the decline of cities, while the Mongol administration in China encouraged urban development
H. lacked a well-defined administrative structure, while the Mongol administration in China established a robust bureaucratic system
I. stimulated Russian export of trade goods to China, while the Mongol administration in China intentionally curtailed Chinese economic activity
J. discouraged inter-cultural exchanges, while the Mongol administration in China promoted cultural integration

According to Hakim (1995, 1996), the majority of part-time work is done by women because:

A. full time work is not available in the kinds of occupations that they want to enter
B. women prefer part-time work as it provides a better work-life balance
C. part-time work offers more flexible hours, allowing women to manage their domestic responsibilities
D. they make a rational choice to combine employment with their 'home-centred' lives
E. women prefer the less demanding nature of part-time work
F. their choices are constrained by structural factors, such as being unable to afford childcare
G. employers prefer to hire women for part-time positions due to societal stereotypes
H. part-time work allows women to have more time for personal development and hobbies
I. there are more part-time job opportunities available for women
J. part-time jobs pay relatively high wages and so are financially beneficial

In relation to the rib, the corresponding intercostal nerve lies

A. medial to its inferior border.
B. medial to its superior border.
C. lateral to its inferior border.
D. deep to its lateral border.
E. superficial to its lateral border.

A 42-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 10 minutes after being involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which she was a restrained passenger. On arrival, she has shortness of breath and abdominal pain. Her pulse is 135/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 80/40 mm Hg. Breath sounds are decreased at the left lung base. An x-ray of the chest shows opacification of the left lower lung field with loss of the diaphragmatic shadow. Placement of a chest tube yields a small amount of air followed by greenish fluid. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A. Immediate intubation
B. Administration of supplemental oxygen
C. Thoracotomy
D. Intravenous administration of broad-spectrum antibiotics
E. CT scan of the chest
F. Laparotomy
G. Placement of a second chest tube
H. CT scan of the abdomen
I. Thoracoscopy
J. Repeat chest x-ray

A. Nearly one-third
B. Roughly 90%
C. Almost all
D. Less than 10%
E. About 15%
F. About 25%
G. Nearly half
H. Approximately 60%
I. About 75%
J. More than 50%

A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-day history of fever, severe muscle pain, and diffuse, painful swelling of his neck, underarms, and groin area. The symptoms began after returning from a camping trip in New Mexico. He appears ill and lethargic and can barely answer questions. His temperature is 39.2°C (102.5°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows generalized scattered black maculae. Examination of the right upper extremity shows an erythematous, solid, tender mass on the underside of the upper extremity just above the elbow; the mass is draining blood and necrotic material. The most effective antibiotic for this patient’s disorder will interfere with which of the following cellular processes or enzymes?

A. Ribosomal assembly
B. RNA polymerase activity
C. Mitochondrial ATP synthesis
D. Glucuronosyltransferase
E. DNA helicase
F. Proteasomal degradation
G. Phospholipase function
H. Lipid synthesis
I. Topoisomerase II activity
J. Cell wall synthesis

A 65-year-old female is admitted to the hospital after experiencing aphasia and right-sided hemiparesis. She subsequently develops urinary incontinence. There is no evidence of urinary tract infection and no prior history of urinary pathology. The most likely diagnosis is

A. autonomic neurogenic bladderv
B. Detrusor underactivity
C. Overactive bladder
D. uninhibited neurogenic bladder
E. Functional incontinence
F. Overflow incontinence
G. motor paralytic bladder
H. Stress incontinence
I. Spastic neurogenic bladder

A. Transmitted by females only to males
B. Transmitted by males only to females
C. Female to female transmission
D. No male to male or female to female transmission
E. Male to male transmission
F. Parental consanguinity
G. Male to female transmission, but not female to male
H. Transmission only by males
I. Both parents must carry the gene
J. Transmission only by females

D. Upward sloping
E. Vertical
F. Zigzag
G. Downward sloping
H. Exponentially increasing
I. Parabolic

A. unemployment will rise.
B. taxes will have to be raised.
C. the equilibrium price level will rise.
D. government spending on important programs will have to be cut.
